pull.title: refactor: Enhance type safety in overflow operations
pull.body:
* Correct copy-paste error in RPC code
* Require proper integers for `SaturatingAdd()` and `AdditionOverflow()` in src/util/overflow.h

These changes increase the type safety of the code and were done while exploring increasing the type-safety of `CAmount` (currently just a `typedef` of `int64_t`).

The first commit has nothing to do with overflow but is along for the ride if reviewers agree.

verification.payload:
tree 9e2eb88ee5a055ff705f891f907a731999e4f89c
parent a815e3e2629fdcc1331a070a574fba595dac5c9e
author Hodlinator <172445034+hodlinator@users.noreply.github.com> 1776956296 +0200
committer Hodlinator <172445034+hodlinator@users.noreply.github.com> 1779730509 +0200

util: Require integers for SaturatingAdd() and AdditionOverflow()

Previously we could fall back to using an unspecialized implementation of std::numeric_limits<T> which would compile as long as the numeric operators existed, but would return 0 for min() & max().

events.body: The following sections might be updated with supplementary metadata relevant to reviewers and maintainers. ### Code Coverage & Benchmarks For details see: https://corecheck.dev/bitcoin/bitcoin/pulls/35372. ### Reviews See [the guideline](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md#code-review) for information on the review process. | Type | Reviewers | | ---- | --------- | | ACK | [maflcko](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/35372#issuecomment-4552892420), [winterrdog](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/35372#pullrequestreview-4371271218), [sedited](https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/35372#pullrequestreview-4371381892) | If your review is incorrectly listed, please copy-paste <!--meta-tag:bot-skip--> into the comment that the bot should ignore.

approach ACK

using c++20 concepts here is a good move to enforce _strict_ type safety
Latest push removes the last commit disallowing booleans in overflow.h. Should be less controversial.

ACK 0774eaaf0c221b3fed68e866130abafa2890880c

the updated diff drops the custom constraints that disallowed `bool`eans

comments.diff_hunk:
@@ -15,6 +15,7 @@
 
 template <std::integral T>
 [[nodiscard]] bool AdditionOverflow(const T i, const T j) noexcept
+    requires(!std::is_same_v<T, bool>)
comments.body:
Instead of adding `requires` to each utility, would it be preferable to define a simple concept that is `std::integral` but without the `bool` type (ditto `std::unsigned_integral`)?

```cpp
template <typename T>
concept non_bool_integral =
    std::integral<std::remove_cvref_t<T>> &&
    !std::same_as<std::remove_cvref_t<T>, bool>;
```

comments.diff_hunk:
@@ -15,6 +15,7 @@
 
 template <std::integral T>
 [[nodiscard]] bool AdditionOverflow(const T i, const T j) noexcept
+    requires(!std::is_same_v<T, bool>)
comments.body:
agree with this direction

i wonder if it may be even cleaner to keep the `bool` exclusion itself as the reusable/composable concept, instead of coupling it directly into `non_bool_integral`.

sth like:

```c++
template <typename T>
concept not_bool =
    !std::same_as<std::remove_cvref_t<T>, bool>;
```

then compose it where needed, for instance:

```c++
requires std::integral<T> && not_bool<T>
```

the reason i slightly prefer this direction is that `not_bool` feels like an orthogonal semantic property rather than something inherently tied to `integral`s specifically.

it also scales a bit better _compositionally_ since some call sites may only care about excluding `bool`, while others may want combinations like:

```c++
std::unsigned_integral<T> && not_bool<T>
```

or other constraints later on.

that way the concepts can stay small and reusable, while the constraints read closer to their actual intent.

keen to hear what you think about this approach.

comments.diff_hunk:
@@ -35,14 +35,16 @@ template <class T>
 
 template <std::unsigned_integral T, std::unsigned_integral U>
 [[nodiscard]] constexpr bool TrySub(T& i, const U j) noexcept
+    requires(!std::is_same_v<T, bool> && !std::is_same_v<U, bool>)
comments.body:
Seems fine, but is there anything wrong here? Looking at the diff, using `bool` seems used and perfectly fine:

```diff
-        Assume(TrySub(m_dirty_count, it->second.IsDirty()));
+        Assume(TrySub(m_dirty_count, unsigned{it->second.IsDirty()}));
```

And about the other changes in this file: I can't really see bool to be used here in a buggy way, and if there was an unlikely case where it really was used, it would fail at runtime.

No objection, but all of this seems a bit verbose, absent a real (past) bug or imaginary bug that this could be catching?

comments.diff_hunk:
@@ -15,6 +15,7 @@
 
 template <std::integral T>
 [[nodiscard]] bool AdditionOverflow(const T i, const T j) noexcept
+    requires(!std::is_same_v<T, bool>)
comments.body: Thanks for the feedback and example code. While it does feel weird to allow booleans here, I think I'll save that battle for another time if I get stronger ammunition for advocating it.

comments.diff_hunk:
@@ -35,14 +35,16 @@ template <class T>
 
 template <std::unsigned_integral T, std::unsigned_integral U>
 [[nodiscard]] constexpr bool TrySub(T& i, const U j) noexcept
+    requires(!std::is_same_v<T, bool> && !std::is_same_v<U, bool>)
comments.body: Fair. Deferring disallowing booleans for now.
